coredata.dc:title: A rapid room-temperature self-healing, antibacterial, photoluminescent waterborne polyurethane coating with excellent mechanical properties based on double dynamic cross-linked network used for leather finishing
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yupeng
Jin, Yong
Zhou, Rong
Zeng, Wenhua
Mei, Jiangyang
coredata.dc:description: Waterborne polyurethane that integrates fast room-temperature self-healable ability, high mechanical properties, antibacterial activity, and photoluminescence features is highly desirable but challenging. Herein, a novel waterborne polyurethane (ZIWPU) is synthesized with the double dynamic cross-linked networks of multiple hydrogen bonds formed by asymmetric polyurea structure and Zn2+-carboxylate coordination bonds. Profiting from the synergistic effects of double dynamic cross-linked networks, the optimized ZIWPU2 film exhibits excellent mechanical properties (tensile strength of 34.36 MPa and toughness of 107.21 MJ/m3) and rapid room-temperature self-healing ability (healing efficiency > 85 % within 10 h) with the assistant of trace alcohol, which is superior to those of the most of currently reported room-temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethane. In addition, owing to the existence of Zn2+, ZIWPU2 is simultaneously integrated with the outstanding antibacterial ability (antibacterial rate>96 %). Especially, the carbon dots (CDs) are introduced into ZIWPU2 to give it an excellent photoluminescence function that can be used for anti-counterfeiting and crack diagnosis. Finally, this versatile WPU material is successfully applied to leather finishing.

head.ce:title: A rapid room-temperature self-healing, antibacterial, photoluminescent waterborne polyurethane coating with excellent mechanical properties based on double dynamic cross-linked network used for leather finishing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Waterborne polyurethane that integrates fast room-temperature self-healable ability, high mechanical properties, antibacterial activity, and photoluminescence features is highly desirable but challenging. Herein, a novel waterborne polyurethane (ZIWPU) is synthesized with the double dynamic cross-linked networks of multiple hydrogen bonds formed by asymmetric polyurea structure and Zn2+-carboxylate coordination bonds. Profiting from the synergistic effects of double dynamic cross-linked networks, the optimized ZIWPU2 film exhibits excellent mechanical properties (tensile strength of 34.36 MPa and toughness of 107.21 MJ/m3) and rapid room-temperature self-healing ability (healing efficiency > 85 % within 10 h) with the assistant of trace alcohol, which is superior to those of the most of currently reported room-temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethane. In addition, owing to the existence of Zn2+, ZIWPU2 is simultaneously integrated with the outstanding antibacterial ability (antibacterial rate>96 %). Especially, the carbon dots (CDs) are introduced into ZIWPU2 to give it an excellent photoluminescence function that can be used for anti-counterfeiting and crack diagnosis. Finally, this versatile WPU material is successfully applied to leather finishing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Leather is widely favored for its gorgeous and fashionable appearance, soft and delicate feel, and excellent wearing comfort. The leather products need to be finished during the production process to protect them from damage and hide any defects or irregular appearance [1,2]. In recent years, waterborne polyurethane (WPU) leather finishes have been widely used, because of the restrictive environmental regulations on volatile organic chemicals (VOCs) [3,4]. Nevertheless, the leather WPU coating inevitably meets physical damage such as scratches and microcracks during use, which weakens their protective function and affects their appearance. To improve the durability and reliability of WPU coating, endowing waterborne polyurethane with repair function, especially at room temperature, is a promising way, taking into account the denaturation of leather collagen fibers at high temperatures [5–7].

To obtain self-healing functions, various dynamic covalent bonds or non-covalent interactions (e.g., disulfide bonds [8], D-A bonds [9], boroxine bonds [10], ionic interactions [11], hydrogen bonds [12], and coordination bonds [13]) have been utilized to cross-link polymer chains to construct the reversible network. Strong crosslinked bonds can increase the rigidity of polymer networks and improve their mechanical strength, but seriously limit their self-healing ability, while weak ones have the opposite effect [14,15]. As a result, there is always an intrinsic contradiction between mechanical property and healing ability [16]. To balance the contradiction, researchers have incorporated dual dynamic cross-linking interactions into a polymer network, in which the stronger dynamic bonds can fix the network structure, affording sufficient mechanical robustness, while the weak dynamic bonds act as reversible sacrificial bonds, giving polymers fine toughness and self-healing performance [17,18]. Recently, Duan et al. [17] synthesized a dual crosslinked polyurethane network containing D-A bonds and symmetry multiple hydrogen bonds formed by double acyl amino-urea motifs through a polyaddition reaction and a cross-linking reaction in dimethylacetamide (DMAc) solution. When the external force was applied to the material, weaker multiple hydrogen bonds could be preferentially broken to dissipate the energy of the system, while stronger D-A bonds could fix the relative positions of molecular chains, thus effectively maintaining the stability of the three-dimensional cross-linking network. Meanwhile, their dynamic reversibility at high temperatures imparted excellent repairable performances to the resulting polyurethane elastomers. However, the strong D-A bond could only be dissociated at high temperatures, and the regular multiple hydrogen bonds, especially with a high density of regular multiple hydrogen bonds, could easily lead to a regular arrangement of chain segments, inducing the formation of crystallization. Therefore, its repair temperature of 120 °C was far higher than room temperature. Besides, Xu et al. introduced regular quadruple H-bonding 2-ureido-4[1H] pyrimidinone (UPy) units as a chain extender into the side chain to prepare the PU DMAc solution and then added zinc chloride DMAc solution to prepare the room-temperature self-healable polyurethane containing multiple hydrogen bonds and coordination bonds. The resulting PU possessed a strong strength of 14.15 MPa and a high toughness of 47.57 MJ/m3. However, it took a long time (24 h) to achieve healing with 95 % healing efficiency and involved the use and release of a large number of organic solvents [18]. Most importantly, the method of directly adding metal salt DMAc solution to form coordination bond cross-linking is not suitable for aqueous polyurethane systems, because the metal salt has a strong interaction with the hydrophilic carboxyl group on the surface of the emulsion particles, resulting in the aggregation of emulsion particles and the formation of gelatinous substances. Therefore, how to introduce dual dynamic crosslinking networks into waterborne polymers is an urgent problem to be solved.

Currently, only a very limited number of articles have reported on room-temperature self-healable WPU cross-linked by double dynamic bonds. Recently, Lei et al. [19] incorporated branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) into anionic WPU emulsion to prepare room-temperature self-healable dual cross-linked WPU (CWPU). The ionic bonds between amino groups in the PEI molecular structure and carboxy groups of WPU and intrinsic hydrogen bonds could effectively improve the mechanical performances of the material. The tensile strength and elongation at break of CWPU could reach 17.12 MPa and 512.25 %, respectively. However, due to its highly branching structure, the cross-linking density of the CWPU network was high, which greatly restricted the mobility of the molecular chains. Consequently, the healing efficiency of CWPU was even as low as 19 % within 24 h at 30 °C, which makes it difficult to satisfy the requirements of practical use. Although there has been some progress in the preparation of dual dynamic cross-linked self-healable WPU networks, more feasible manners for the construction of dynamic cross-linked networks in WPU matrix to balance the mechanical robustness and healing performances at room-temperature are still in seeking encouraged by the increasing application demands.

In addition, the integration of antimicrobial and photoluminescence functions in the leather coating can further improve its protection level and added value. As is well known, the antimicrobial function can prevent damage to the appearance and the degradation of physical and mechanical properties of leather coating caused by bacterial erosion [20,21]. Recently, photoluminescence technology has been cleverly used for crack diagnosis [22,23], which avoids the propagation and accumulation of cracks that cause macroscopic cracks inside the material and ultimately lead to catastrophic material failure. However, their fluorescence functions were achieved by aggregation induction of special monomers. The synthesis of these monomers required expensive raw materials and complex processes. Additionally, the photoluminescence function can also achieve the anti-counterfeiting of materials.

Herein, we first design a healable WPU with a good trade-off between mechanical strength and healing efficiency through the synergistic dual dynamic interactions of high-density irregular multiple hydrogen bonds and Zn2+-carboxyl coordination bonds. The specific strategy is shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, IWPU was synthesized by introducing polyurea structures into a polyurethane framework through the post-chain extension reaction of isophorone diamine (IPDA) (Fig. 1a). The unsymmetrical structure of IPDA hindered the ordered arrangement of multiple hydrogen bonds formed by urea groups, which inhibited the formation of crystallization, thereby improving the self-healing rate and efficiency of materials. Then, zinc ammonia complex ([Zn(NH3)4]2+) buffer solution was added to the IWPU emulsion and mixed well to fabricate the ZIWPU emulsion. When the metal ions existed as ammonia coordination complex ions, the ligand NH3 could protect the metal ions to slow down the complexation of the metal ions and the carboxyl groups on the polyurethane chain, so that the waterborne polyurethane emulsion was in a stable state. During the film-forming process, as ammonia volatilized, free zinc ions were released to coordinate with the carboxyl groups on polyurethane chains, while asymmetric urea groups could form multiple hydrogen bonds (Fig. 1b).

The dual non-covalent crosslinking effect of the ZIWPU network gave it excellent mechanical properties and self-healable capacity, whose optimized tensile strength and toughness were as high as ca. 34.36 MPa and 107.21 MJ/m3, respectively. With the help of trace ethanol, the tensile strength of damaged ZIWPU2 could recover to more than 85 % of the original sample within 10 h at room temperature. Its comprehensive properties surpassed the majority of currently reported room-temperature self-healable WPU materials (Table S1). When ZIWPU was applied to leather finishing, the introduction of dynamic coordination and multiple hydrogen bonds into the leather coating system could give the coating an excellent self-healing function together with improved adhesion and hydrophobic properties. Meanwhile, Zn2+ endowed WPU coating with excellent resistance to E. coli and S. aureus. Importantly, carbon dots (CDs) were incorporated into the WPU matrices to achieve their photoluminescence properties and demonstrate their crack diagnosis and anti-counterfeiting functions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of waterborne polyurethane containing IPDA (IWPU) and neat waterborne polyurethane (NWPU)
ce:section.ce:para: Waterborne polyurethanes containing different IPDA contents were synthesized, and their recipes are listed in Table S2. The obtained WPU emulsion was marked as IWPUx, where x (1, 2, and 3) represented the IPDA molar percent content (4.3, 9.0, and 13.3 mol%). Taking IWPU2 as an example, its synthesis route is shown in Fig. S1, and the detailed synthesis procedure can be found in the supporting materials. Similar to the synthesis process of IWPU2, neat waterborne polyurethane (NWPU) was synthesized using equimolar BDO instead of IPDA and used as a control sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of IWPU2 emulsions and films containing Zn2+(ZIWPU)
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of IWPU2 emulsion containing Zn2+ is similar to the method we reported earlier [13]. The IWPU2 emulsion containing Zn2+ was named ZIWPUy, where y (1, 2, and 3) represented the molar ratio of Zn2+ to the carboxyl group in IWPU2 (1/12, 1/6, and 1/4). Taking ZIWPU2 as an example, a zinc ammonia complex ([Zn(NH3)4] 2+) buffer solution was first prepared by dissolving 0.055 g of zinc acetate in 7 g of deionized water and adding 1 g of ammonia. After that, the [Zn(NH3)4]2+ buffer solution was slowly added dropwise to 15 g of IWPU2 emulsion under the action of stirring (150 rpm) and mixed evenly for 30 min. Finally, the resulting compound emulsion was poured into the PTFE mold and dried for one week at room temperature, followed by drying at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain a film with a thickness of about 0.5 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) and composite films (WCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: First, the CDS were prepared according to the previously reported method with slight modifications [24]. 0.5 mL of ethylenediamine and 20 mL of ethyl acetate were mixed evenly, then added to a 50 mL stainless steel reactor, and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was evaporated at 40 °C to obtain CDS. 1 g of CDS was first dissolved in 5 g of deionized water to prepare the CDS solution. Adding 100, 200, 400, and 800 μL of carbon quantum dot solution into 10 g ZIWPU2 emulsion to obtain composite emulsions (WCDs), which were labeled as WCDs1, WCDs2, WCDs3, and WCDs4, respectively. Then, the WCDs emulsions were poured into the PTFE molds, dried at room temperature for one week, and further dried at 60 °C for 24 h. Herein, ZIWPU2 was renamed as WCDs0 for easy comparison.

Before coating the leather, the required emulsion content in the leather sample area needed to be calculated according to QB/T 2002.2–2018. A certain amount of polyurethane emulsion was applied repeatedly and evenly to the leather surface using a brush.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emulsion properties and chemical structure of the WPU films
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different IPDA and Zn2+ dosages on the average particle size, zeta potential, appearance, and centrifugal stability of WPU emulsions were first investigated. As shown in Fig. 2a and Table S3, the NWPU, IWPUx, and ZIWPUy emulsions exhibit an average particle size ranging from 41.06 to 54.22 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) from 0.16 to 0.21. In addition, the absolute value of the zeta potential of all emulsions is greater than 30 [25,26]. As shown in Fig. 2b, the morphology of the ZIWPU2 emulsion particles observed by AFM is approximately spherical. In addition, no precipitation is found in all WPU emulsions, indicating good centrifugation stability (Fig. S2). To sum up, the above results indicate that the particle size distribution of WPU emulsions is narrow and has good dispersion stability, which is crucial for subsequent applications.

After the evaporation of water in the above emulsions, a series of films with different IPDA or Zn2+ contents were successfully prepared. The chemical structure of the synthesized WPU films was initially determined using FT-IR. The information on general characteristic peaks of WPU was analyzed and the results can be found in the supporting materials. Regarding the different infrared spectra, the difference is mainly located at about 1670 cm−1, corresponding to the tensile vibration of the urea carbonyl group [27]. With the increase of IPDA content, the absorption peak of the urea carbonyl group is enhanced (Fig. 2c). This is because more amino groups derived from IPDA react with isocyanate groups on the prepolymer. Compared with IWPU2, the spectrum of ZIWPU3 shows a significant absorption peak at about 1630 cm−1, indicating the presence of Zn2+-carboxyl coordination bonds in the polymer matrix (Fig. 2d) [28]. Additionally, the mapping elemental profiles of the ZIWPU2 were used to clarify the uniform distribution of Zn2+ in the polymer matrix (Fig. 2e).

As shown in Fig. S3, all XRD curves of NWPU, IWPU2, and ZIWPU2 only display wider diffraction peaks at about 20°, indicating all polyurethane networks are the amorphous structures, which is closely related to the fact that the asymmetric structure of IPDA hinders the orderly arrangement of hydrogen bonds and inhibits the regular aggregation of chain segments to form crystallization. The dynamic thermal properties of WPU films were investigated using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) heating curves. As shown in Fig. S4, the Tg values of NWPU, IWPU2, and ZIWPU2 are −78.8 °C, −75.6 °C and –73.4 °C, respectively, which are significantly lower than room temperature, indicating that the polymer chains have excellent motility at room temperature, which favors their room-temperature self-healing. Furthermore, the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) results of NWPU, IWPU2, and ZIWPU2 suggest that they have excellent thermal stability, with the T5% above 260 °C (Fig. S5 and Table S4) [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of NWPU, IWPU and ZIWPU films
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effect of IPDA on mechanical properties, WPU specimens were subjected to uniaxial tensile tests. The representative stress-strain profiles are depicted in Fig. 3a, and the corresponding mechanical properties are listed in Table S5. The histograms of strength and elongation are shown in Fig. 3b, which more intuitively reflect the changes in mechanical properties. For NWPU, since the sample only contains weak intrinsic hydrogen bonds, it shows a tensile strength of 4.51 MPa and a strain at break of 1061 %. When the content of IPDA reaches 4.3 mol%, the sample exhibits a tensile strength of 15.45 MPa, which is about 3.5 times that of NWPU, while the strain at break slightly decreases from 1061 % to 927 %. With the content of IPDA further increasing to 9.0 mol%, the tensile strength improves to 23.42 MPa for IWPU2, and the strain at break declines to 866 %. Although IWPU3 exhibits a higher tensile strength of 30.12 MPa when the IPDA content is increased to 13.3 mol%, its elongation at break dramatically declines to 794 %. The significant improvement in tensile strength is related to the formation of multiple hydrogen bonds. Specifically, the FTIR spectra of NWPU, IWPU1, IWPU2, and IWPU3 in the CO area (1600-1750 cm−1) with peak deconvolution are displayed to reveal the internal hydrogen bonding structures (Fig. 3e). The CO absorption area of all samples was deconvoluted into four subpeaks, where I and III represent free CO of carbamate and urea, while II and IV represent hydrogen-bonded (H-bonded) CO [30,31]. Origin 2021 performed area integration of the four subpeaks and calculated their proportions for quantifying the proportion of H-bonded CO (Table S6). The results show that the proportions of H-bonded CO in the NWPU, IWPU1, IWPU2, and IWPU3 networks are 52.4 %, 55.9 %, 58.7 %, and 62.9 %, respectively, which indicates that more amino groups derived from IPDA react with the isocyanate groups of prepolymers to form more urea groups, which are involved in the formation of a high density of hydrogen bonds. Subsequently, AFM height images of NWPU, IWPU2, and IWPU3 are compared in Fig. 3f to illustrate changes in the morphology of the microphase separation, where hard segments are shown as bright areas and soft segments are shown as dark areas [32]. The microphase separation structure of NWPU is not obvious, and the hard and soft segments are evenly mixed. Compared with NWPU, the light and dark areas in the AFM topography image of IWPU2 are slightly obvious, indicating that the phase separation degree of IWPU2 is a little higher than that of NWPU. This reveals that an appropriately high density multiple hydrogen bonds formed by polyurea structure enhance the interaction of hard segments, but do not cause tight accumulation or form crystallization due to the asymmetric six-membered aliphatic ring structure of IPDA. However, when the IPDA content reaches 13.3 mol%, the microphase separation structure of IWPU3 becomes apparent, and the number of hard segments sharply increases. The results suggest that the obvious increase of urea groups makes the cross-linking mode of the hard domain change from the relatively loose hybrid urethane/urea hydrogen bond cross-linking to the more tight-packed urea hydrogen bond cross-linking. Therefore, the excessive increase in IPDA content undoubtedly results in a significant increase in the number of hydrogen bonds and hard domain content, which act as high-density crosslinking sites to effectively strengthen the interaction between polymer chains, but unavoidably reduce the unfolding and sliding of molecular chains. Therefore, it is difficult to achieve a further obvious increase in strength through individual adjustment of IPDA content without sharply affecting the chain mobility.

To this end, we attempted to introduce a small number of stronger coordination interactions in the IWPU2 network to achieve the same strengthening effect as a greater number of weak hydrogen bonds, while having less effect on the mobility of segments due to the smaller crosslinking density. The typical stress-strain curves of ZIWPUy are depicted in Fig. 3c and corresponding mechanical parameters are shown in Fig. 3d and Table S5. When the molar ratio of Zn2+/-COOH increases from 0 to 1/6, the tensile strength significantly increases from 23.42 MPa for IWPU2 to 34.36 MPa for ZIWPU2 with minor changes of elongation at break. The improvement in material tensile strength can be ascribed to the formation of Zn2+-carboxylate coordination bonds in the polymer matrix (Fig. 2d,e). The AFM topography of ZIWPU2 was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3f, although the ZIWPU2 has a more pronounced phase separation morphology than IWPU2, its hard domain morphology is significantly different from that of IWPU3. IWPU3 exhibits dense and small hard domains, while ZIWPU2 exhibits sparse and large hard domains. The reason is that coordination bonds lead to relatively larger aggregates of hard domain compared to hydrogen bonds due to their stronger bond energy. Fortunately, due to the smaller number of hard segment aggregates, their impact on the soft segment is relatively weak compared to IWPU3. However, when the molar ratio of Zn2+/-COOH is further increased to 1/4, the tensile strength of ZIWPU3 decreases to 30.51 MPa. To further explore the reasons for this situation, we also performed peak fitting on the carbonyl regions in the infrared spectra of ZIWPU2 and ZIWPU3. As shown in Fig. S6 and Table S7, with the introduction of zinc ions, the proportion of bonded carbonyl groups at about 1345 cm−1 increases dramatically from 10.6 % to 35.1 %, which is the result of the formation of new coordination bonds by zinc ions and carboxyl groups. When the molar ratio of Zn2+/-COOH is further increased to 1/4, the proportion of bonded carbonyl groups at about 1345 cm−1 increases to 56.3 %, indicating that more coordination bonds are formed. However, the proportion of H-bonded CO from urethane reduces significantly from 36.3 % to 26.4 % and the proportion of free amino groups increases from 18.5 % to 22.3 %. The results show that zinc ions can form coordination bonds with the carbonyl groups, but this inhibits the formation of hydrogen bonds between the amino and the carbonyl groups because the metal salt is a stronger electron acceptor than NH [33]. Since the side effect of reducing hydrogen bonding is greater than the positive effect of increasing coordination bonds, the strength of the material is reduced. Compared with ZIWPU3, ZIWPU2 contains more weak hydrogen bonds, and their cleavage and recombination during stretching contribute to more adequate chain extension [14,34]. As a result, the elongation at break of ZIWPU2 (853 %) is significantly better than that of ZIWPU3 (805 %). Therefore, neither an excess of hydrogen bonds nor an excess of coordination bonds in the WPU network can achieve an optimal solution. This is also the reason why there is not much difference in the mechanical properties of ZIWPU3 and IWPU3. The above analysis verifies that the appropriate amount of coordination bonds and multiple hydrogen bonds can exert synergistic effects to improve the mechanical strength and toughness of materials.

To investigate the toughening mechanism, cyclic tensile tests were performed. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the cyclic tensile profiles of NWPU, IWPU2, and ZIWPU2 exhibit obvious hysteresis loops, suggesting energy is dissipated during cycling through the fracturing of dynamic bonds. As shown in Fig. 4b, the toughness is only 1.92 MJ/m3 for NWPU, which mainly depends on the disrupting of inherent weak carbamate hydrogen bonds for dissipating energy. After introducing IPDA groups, the toughness value of IWPU2 reaches 6.28 MJ/m3, which is about 3.27 times that of NWPU, illustrating that the newly generated multiple urea hydrogen bonds can also participate in energy dissipation as sacrificial bonds. Compared with IWPU2, the toughness of ZIWPU2 is increased by 53 % to 9.66 MJ/m3. The further obvious increase in toughness is caused by the partial breakage of the coordination bonds during the cycling process. In addition, the stretch-recovery behaviors under different elongations (100 %, 200 %, 400 %, and 600 %) were also tested with ZIWPU2 as the example. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the hysteresis loop area increases with increasing strain, indicating that more energy is dissipated during the stretching process due to the dissociation of more dynamic bonds. In addition, it can be observed from Fig. 4d that as the strain increases from 100 % to 600 %, ZIWPU2 can dissipate more apparent energy with an increasing energy dissipation rate. In detail, when the strain is 100 % or 200 %, ZIWPU2 can dissipate energy as low as 0.51 MJ/m3 or 1.85 MJ/m3 with a lower energy dissipation rate of 34.2 % or 42.3 %, respectively. The energy dissipation rate of ZIWPU2 is not so large at 100 % or 200 % strain because it mainly relies on weak hydrogen bond breakage to dissipate energy in the low tensile range. When the strain is increased to 400 %, the effective energy dissipation of ZIWPU2 is as high as 9.66 MJ/m3, and the corresponding energy dissipation ratio reaches 61.9 %. The reason is due to the dissociation of a large number of relatively weak multiple hydrogen bonds and some strong coordination interactions. When the strain reaches 600 %, as more strong coordination bonds break, the dissipation energy and dissipation ratio of ZIWPU reaches 33.48 MJ/m3 and 73 %, respectively. In addition, when the sample is completely fractured, the fracture toughness values of NWPU, IWPU2, and ZIWPU2 are 18.46 MJ/m3, 68.59 MJ/m3, and 107.21 MJ/m3 (Fig. 4e), respectively. This is because the energy dissipation of the system is the effect of the synergy of the intrinsic urethane hydrogen bonds, relatively weak multiple urea hydrogen bonds, and relatively strong Zn2+-carboxyl coordination bonds. A repeated tensile cyclic test was performed at a strain of 400 % using ZIWPU2 as an example to demonstrate its recoverability. As depicted in Fig. 4f, the large hysteresis loop in the first cycle illustrates that the sample dissipates energy clearly by breaking dynamic cross-linking bonds. It can be observed that the hysteresis circle area of the sample in the second load-unload cycle is significantly smaller than that in the first loading cycle. This is because the dynamic bonds that break on the first load do not have enough time to return to their original state [35]. As the number of cycles increases, the area of the hysteresis loop decreases slightly, indicating that the break and recombination of the sacrificial bond are in equilibrium [36]. When the sample is relaxed at room temperature for 24 h and loaded-unloaded in the 11th cycle, the curve is similar to the first cycle, indicating that the broken dynamic bonds are almost completely restored. Moreover, when the strain is greater than 400 %, the slope of the ZIWPU2 stress-strain profile increases sharply, which is a manifestation of the strain reinforcement behavior (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 4g, when the WPU spline is stretched to 600 %, the sample shows signs of whiting, which is due to the formation of the crystalline phase. When relaxed for 1 min, the spline reverts to its original appearance (Fig. S8), indicating that the formation of the crystalline phase is reversible and mainly affected by strain. The XRD was used to further reveal the changes in its microstructure. The unstretched ZIWPU2 exhibits a broad amorphous diffraction peak at about 20°, suggesting that the network structure is almost completely disordered without any crystallization zone (Fig. S3). When ZIWPU2 is stretched to 600 %, crystalline peaks of PTMG are observed at about 20° and 24° (Fig. 4h) [37]. After 1 min of relaxation, the characteristic peaks of PTMG crystallization in its XRD curve disappear (Fig. S8). Given the above analysis, we propose a reasonable toughening mechanism for the synthetic dual dynamic cross-linked WPU network. In the case of small strain, weak intrinsic urethane hydrogen bonds, relatively weak multiple urea hydrogen bonds, as well as some relatively strong coordination bonds can be broken to effectively dissipate the energy of the system. During the large tensile procedure, more strong coordination bonds can be broken down to dissipate more energy. Meanwhile, hierarchical dynamic actions break sequentially during the tensile process, so that the PTMG chains are fully extended and arranged in an orderly manner along the tensile direction to form crystals, inducing additional self-reinforcing and self-toughening effects (Fig. 4i).

We focused on the room-temperature healing properties of ZIWPU2 due to its excellent tensile stress and toughness. As shown in Fig. S9, the scratches on the surface of the ZIWPU2 film do not exhibit significant healing behavior within 24 h at room temperature. In addition, the tensile strength of ZIWPU2 only reaches 2.82 MPa and the repair efficiency is as low as 8.2 % (Fig. S10). To overcome this dilemma, the ethanol-induced self-healing strategy we previously reported was employed [12,13]. The self-healing procedure is shown in Fig. 5a. The dumbbell-shaped sample was cut in half from the middle with a blade, and then the two fracture surfaces were immediately bonded together. Additionally, 3 μL of ethanol was dripped onto the scar to aid healing. It is important to note that trace amounts of ethanol do not affect the dimensional stability of the specimen. Optical micrographs show that the damage is eliminated after the sample is repaired at room temperature for 10 h (Fig. 5b). As can be seen from Fig. 5c, the healed specimen is stretched to 700 % without breaking. In addition, a round ZIWPU2 sample (4 cm in diameter, 1 mm in thickness) can hang an 8 kg dumbbell after being soaked in ethanol for 30 s and healing within 10 h, indicating that the healed ZIWPU2 sample also has ultra-high mechanical strength (Fig. 5d and Movie S1), in which the two samples are painted blue and pink respectively for easy distinction. The recovery ability of mechanical properties can quantitatively describe the self-healing efficiency of samples. As depicted in Fig. S11a, the healing efficiency of NWPU is as low as 57.6 %, because weak intrinsic hydrogen bonds in the polyurethane matrix are insufficient to maintain the strength of the network. The healing efficiency of IWPU2 increases significantly and reaches 90.3 % after the introduction of 9.0 mol% IPDA (Fig. S11b). The obvious improvement in healing efficiency is ascribed to the newly formed irregular multiple urea hydrogen bonds that participated in the repair procedure at the fracture surface. Meanwhile, the asymmetric ring structure of IPDA disturbs the regular arrangement of multiple hydrogen bonds and avoids the formation of crystallization, resulting in amorphous polymers, which is conducive to the mobility of molecular chains (Fig. 3e). However, when the amount of IPDA is further increased to 13.3 mol%, the healing efficiency of IWPU3 decreases to 81.4 % (Fig. S11c). This is because too many hydrogen bond cross-linking sites severely limit the movement of the chain segments. When a small amount of zinc ions is introduced, the healing efficiency of ZIWPU2 (86.2 %) is lower than that of IPDA2 but higher than that of IPDA3. Surprisingly, the tensile stress of the healed ZIWPU2 (29.65 MPa) sample is higher than those of IWPU2 (21.07 MPa) and IWPU3 (24.41 MPa), respectively. The results prove that a small number of strong dynamic coordination bonds in the ZIWPU2 network used to replace more multiple hydrogen bonds can achieve a better effect of strengthening the network, and at the same time, they have less effect on the restriction of chain segment motion (Fig. 3f). Fig. 5e shows the typical stress–strain profiles of the ZIWPU2 sample healed with the aid of ethanol for different lengths of time. As shown in Fig. S12, the tensile strength of the sample recovers by about 38.3 % within 2.5 h. When the healing time is extended to 5 h and 7.5 h, the recovery rates of tensile strength are 64.3 % and 78.4 %, respectively. The results show that the self-healing rate of the sample gradually slows down, which may be related to the partial dynamic bond reconstruction after ethanol volatilization, resulting in a decrease in segment motion. After healing for 10 h, the tensile strength and strain at the break of the sample reach 29.65 MPa and 816 %, respectively, and the healing efficiency is 86.2 %, demonstrating the excellent healing ability of ZIWPU2 at room temperature (Fig. 5f). The histogram can clearly show that the synthesized ZIWPU2 has obvious advantages in terms of comprehensive performance (tensile strength, toughness, and repair time) compared to most other room-temperature self-healing WPUs (Fig. 5g). To further illustrate the important role of ethanol in the self-healing process, we dropped an equal amount of deionized water (3 μL) on the wound site of ZIWPU2 and observed its self-healing behavior. As shown in Fig. S13a, the wounds on the surface of the damaged sample still have clear marks after healing for 10 h at room temperature, the repair efficiency of the damaged ZIWPU2 sample is only 13.3 % (Fig. S13b). The self-healing effect of water-induced damaged ZIWPU2 samples is significantly weaker than that of organic solvent ethanol. Similar results have been reported in other literature [18,38]. However, the reasons for this are not yet, and we speculate that the affinity between inorganic water molecules and the organic WPU is poor, resulting in its slow penetration. Moreover, water molecules cannot effectively destroy the self-complementary hydrogen bonding of waterborne polyurethane, which can be inspired by the fact that NWPU can only swell in water but cannot dissolve. These factors lead to a decrease in the self-healing efficiency of the material.

We focused on the room-temperature healing properties of ZIWPU2 due to its excellent tensile stress and toughness. As shown in Fig. S9, the scratches on the surface of the ZIWPU2 film do not exhibit significant healing behavior within 24 h at room temperature. In addition, the tensile strength of ZIWPU2 only reaches 2.82 MPa and the repair efficiency is as low as 8.2 % (Fig. S10). To overcome this dilemma, the ethanol-induced self-healing strategy we previously reported was employed [12,13]. The self-healing procedure is shown in Fig. 5a. The dumbbell-shaped sample was cut in half from the middle with a blade, and then the two fracture surfaces were immediately bonded together. Additionally, 3 μL of ethanol was dripped onto the scar to aid healing. It is important to note that trace amounts of ethanol do not affect the dimensional stability of the specimen. Optical micrographs show that the damage is eliminated after the sample is repaired at room temperature for 10 h (Fig. 5b). As can be seen from Fig. 5c, the healed specimen is stretched to 700 % without breaking. In addition, a round ZIWPU2 sample (4 cm in diameter, 1 mm in thickness) can hang an 8 kg dumbbell after being soaked in ethanol for 30 s and healing within 10 h, indicating that the healed ZIWPU2 sample also has ultra-high mechanical strength (Fig. 5d and Movie S1), in which the two samples are painted blue and pink respectively for easy distinction. The recovery ability of mechanical properties can quantitatively describe the self-healing efficiency of samples. As depicted in Fig. S11a, the healing efficiency of NWPU is as low as 57.6 %, because weak intrinsic hydrogen bonds in the polyurethane matrix are insufficient to maintain the strength of the network. The healing efficiency of IWPU2 increases significantly and reaches 90.3 % after the introduction of 9.0 mol% IPDA (Fig. S11b). The obvious improvement in healing efficiency is ascribed to the newly formed irregular multiple urea hydrogen bonds that participated in the repair procedure at the fracture surface. Meanwhile, the asymmetric ring structure of IPDA disturbs the regular arrangement of multiple hydrogen bonds and avoids the formation of crystallization, resulting in amorphous polymers, which is conducive to the mobility of molecular chains (Fig. 3e). However, when the amount of IPDA is further increased to 13.3 mol%, the healing efficiency of IWPU3 decreases to 81.4 % (Fig. S11c). This is because too many hydrogen bond cross-linking sites severely limit the movement of the chain segments. When a small amount of zinc ions is introduced, the healing efficiency of ZIWPU2 (86.2 %) is lower than that of IPDA2 but higher than that of IPDA3. Surprisingly, the tensile stress of the healed ZIWPU2 (29.65 MPa) sample is higher than those of IWPU2 (21.07 MPa) and IWPU3 (24.41 MPa), respectively. The results prove that a small number of strong dynamic coordination bonds in the ZIWPU2 network used to replace more multiple hydrogen bonds can achieve a better effect of strengthening the network, and at the same time, they have less effect on the restriction of chain segment motion (Fig. 3f). Fig. 5e shows the typical stress–strain profiles of the ZIWPU2 sample healed with the aid of ethanol for different lengths of time. As shown in Fig. S12, the tensile strength of the sample recovers by about 38.3 % within 2.5 h. When the healing time is extended to 5 h and 7.5 h, the recovery rates of tensile strength are 64.3 % and 78.4 %, respectively. The results show that the self-healing rate of the sample gradually slows down, which may be related to the partial dynamic bond reconstruction after ethanol volatilization, resulting in a decrease in segment motion. After healing for 10 h, the tensile strength and strain at the break of the sample reach 29.65 MPa and 816 %, respectively, and the healing efficiency is 86.2 %, demonstrating the excellent healing ability of ZIWPU2 at room temperature (Fig. 5f). The histogram can clearly show that the synthesized ZIWPU2 has obvious advantages in terms of comprehensive performance (tensile strength, toughness, and repair time) compared to most other room-temperature self-healing WPUs (Fig. 5g). To further illustrate the important role of ethanol in the self-healing process, we dropped an equal amount of deionized water (3 μL) on the wound site of ZIWPU2 and observed its self-healing behavior. As shown in Fig. S13a, the wounds on the surface of the damaged sample still have clear marks after healing for 10 h at room temperature, the repair efficiency of the damaged ZIWPU2 sample is only 13.3 % (Fig. S13b). The self-healing effect of water-induced damaged ZIWPU2 samples is significantly weaker than that of organic solvent ethanol. Similar results have been reported in other literature [18,38]. However, the reasons for this are not yet, and we speculate that the affinity between inorganic water molecules and the organic WPU is poor, resulting in its slow penetration. Moreover, water molecules cannot effectively destroy the self-complementary hydrogen bonding of waterborne polyurethane, which can be inspired by the fact that NWPU can only swell in water but cannot dissolve. These factors lead to a decrease in the self-healing efficiency of the material.

Based on the results of the above experiments, we propose a reasonable healing mechanism. As displayed in Fig. 5h, when the ZIWPU2 specimen is damaged, the reversible irregular multiple hydrogen bonds and coordination bonds are simultaneously fractured at the wound. Then, a small amount of ethanol droplets are added to a crack in WPU samples. Herein, ethanol can quickly penetrate the WPU network to fill between the molecular chains and plays a role similar to plasticizers to make the movement of the chain segments easy. Importantly, the ethanol molecule can also further disrupt the self-complementary hydrogen bonds in the WPU network, leaving the carbonyl and amino groups in a dissociated state, which significantly reduces the cross-linking density of the material [12,38,39]. Therefore, a small amount of ethanol can effectively promote the insertion and entanglement of chain segments at the rupture surface, thereby accelerating the rearrangement of the network structure. In addition, with the volatilization of ethanol, relatively weak multiple hydrogen bonds can quickly reconstruct and accelerate the healing process, while the stronger coordination bonds rebuild slower but provide good mechanical strength. The synergistic effect of these factors endows the WPU network with high mechanical properties and rapid self-healing ability [40–42].

Here, thanks to the presence of Zn2+ ions, the synthesized ZIWPUy films are endowed with antimicrobial functions, thus protecting them from bacteria. The typical E. coli and S. aureus were chosen to examine the antibacterial ability of samples. As shown in Fig. 6a, compared with NWPU, IWPU2 with the introduction of the IPDA group does not exhibit obvious antimicrobial activity. Furthermore, the ZIWPUy films exhibit significantly increased antibacterial activity with the increase of zinc ion content. The antibacterial rate (%) of film samples was further calculated to quantitatively characterize their antibacterial performances. When the molar ratio of Zn2+/-COOH is 1/12, the antibacterial rate of ZIWPU1 against E. coli and S. aureus is 83.4 % and 84.6 %, respectively, indicating its good antibacterial capacity. As the molar ratio of Zn2+/-COOH increases from 1/12 to 1/6, the antibacterial rate of ZIWPU2 against E. coli increases to 97.2 %, and the antibacterial rate of S. aureus increases to 98.5 %, implying the antibacterial effect of WPU film is further enhanced (Fig. 6b). As exhibited in Fig. 6c, Zn2+ ions can bind to bacterial cell membranes and membrane proteins, thereby destroying their structure, and then enter the cell to interfere with the cell metabolic processes or disrupt the enzymes in the electron transfer system to achieve antibacterial functions [43,44].

Imparting photoluminescence to WPU films can help the material diagnose surface cracks and prevent counterfeiting. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of fluorescent carbon materials that have attracted attention due to their excellent photoluminescence properties [45,46]. Therefore, we first prepared a carbon dots aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 7a, the aqueous solution of CDs is colorless in the ambient state, while it emits blue fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation (365 nm). As shown in Fig. 7b, the ZIWPU2 film (WCDs0) does not emit light, while the WCDs1-4 films show obvious blue fluorescence under the irradiation of UV light, indicating that the prepared WPU films containing CDs have good photoluminescence function. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of WCDs films increases with increasing CDs content (Fig. 7c). As shown in Fig. 7d, due to the diffuse reflection of light from the rough surface, the fluorescence intensity at the crack is the strongest in the entire film surface under 365 nm irradiation, which is beneficial for us to find cracks. Then, with the addition of a small amount of ethanol, the fluorescence intensity of the crack gradually decreases with the extension of time, indicating that the crack is gradually repaired and disappears. Hence, the combination of ethanol-induced self-healing capacity and photoluminescence function can help to detect microcracks in film and repair them promptly, preventing them from accumulating and causing material failure. The photoluminescence function of WCDs emulsions can also be used for optical anti-counterfeiting. We dipped a brush in the WCDs4 emulsion and wrote the pattern on a non-fluorescent PTFE board. As depicted in Fig. 7e (left), the “I ♡ CHN ” is not visible under natural light, while it emits blue light when irradiated using UV (365 nm). Furthermore, following the procedure shown in Fig. S14, we first dipped a brush in WCDs4 emulsion to write the pattern in Fig. S14a on a non-fluorescent black cardboard. After it was completely dried, we dipped it in WCDS0 emulsion to draw a pattern surrounded by dotted lines in Fig. S14b. When it dried, we obtained the final pattern of Fig. S14 c. As shown in Fig. 7e (right), the black false signal “888” can be observed under natural light. When irradiated under ultraviolet light (365 nm), the true signal “315” manifests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of WPU emulsion in leather finishing
ce:section.ce:para: Leather coatings are inevitably damaged by bumps and scratches during use, affecting the service life and appearance of leather. Therefore, we chose ZIWPU2 with excellent mechanical performance and room-temperature repair ability to form a leather coating. As depicted in Fig. 8a, in contrast to the original leather sample, the finished sample with ZIWPU2 has a smooth surface and still retains the characteristics of softness. To evaluate the waterproofness of the coating, the water contact angles (WCA) of the original and finished leather were first tested. As depicted in Fig. S15, the WCA of the original leather sample gradually decreases over time, and the water completely penetrates the leather after 60 s of contact, which is associated with its highly hydrophilic and porous structure. As expected, finishing can improve the water-resistance of leather, because the WCA of coated leather barely changes over time within 60 s. In detail, the WCA values of NWPU and IWPU2 are 53.1° and 58.7°, respectively (Fig. 8b). The literature reveals that the factors affecting the contact angle of WPU films usually include the polarity of polyols, crosslinking density, hydrophilic group content, and surface microstructure [47–49]. Considering that the compositions of WPU and IWPU have the same hydrophilic group and polyol content, the WCA change of films mainly depends on the difference in crosslinking density and surface microstructure. There is no significant difference in surface morphology between NWPU and IWPU2 (Fig. 3f). Therefore, the main reason is that with the introduction of IPDA, the newly formed multiple hydrogen bonds increase the cross-linking sites between polymer chains. Furthermore, the water contact angle is further increased to 64.6° after zinc ions are introduced into the IWPU2 matrix. The reason may be that zinc ions interact with hydrophilic carboxyl groups on the surface of the WPU film to form a coordinated structure, which weakens the affinity of the carboxyl group with water, and meanwhile increases the crosslinking density. It is worth noting that compared with IWPU2, the microscopic morphology of ZIWPU2 changes significantly, and its surface roughness increases significantly. Therefore, the synergistic effect of these factors improves the hydrophobicity of the film [50,51]. The water resistance of the leather coating was further evaluated by the water absorption ratio (WAR) of WPU films. It is observed from Fig. S16 that the NWPU has a WAR of 9.19 %. For the IWPU2, the WAR decreases to 7.03 %. Also, for IWPU2 loaded with zinc ions (ZIWPU2), the WAR further decreases to 6.08 %. The trend of WAR is consistent with that of contact angle. As a finishing agent, WPU films should adhere tightly to the leather surface to ensure its durability. As shown in Fig. 8c, the adhesion strength of the IWPU2 (0.82 MPa) is higher than that of the NWPU (0.45 MPa). The reason is that more urea groups are formed, which can form more hydrogen bonds with polar groups (−CO − NH−, −OH, −NH2, and − COOH) on leather fibers, thereby enhancing interfacial interactions and allowing energy to be better dissipated under external forces. With the addition of zinc ions to the IWPU2 matrix, the adhesion strength is further improved to 1.21 MPa for ZIWPU2. The reason is that the coordination bond formed inside the WPU matrix strengthens the adhesion layer, and at the same time, the coordination bond is formed at the interface to strengthen the interface interaction. Of course, the self-healing behavior of the leather coating was also observed. As shown in Fig. 8d, when the coating on the leather surface is cratched with a medical blade, a small amount of ethanol is dropped on the wound, and then the scratch on the leather disappears completely at room temperature for 10 min, indicating that the coating formed by the ZIWPU2 emulsion has excellent rapid self-healing with the aid of ethanol. With the introduction of CDs, the leather coating (WCDs4) emits a distinct blue fluorescence when exposed to ultraviolet light, and cracks on its surface can be observed and traced to heal (Fig. 8e).

In summary, we synthesize a novel waterborne polyurethane with dynamic coordination bonds and asymmetric multiple hydrogen bonds double crosslinked networks. Thanks to the synergy of the dual dynamic bonds, the optimized sample (ZIWPU2) exhibits mechanical properties with a tensile stress of 34.36 MPa and toughness of 107.21 MJ/m3, as well as outstanding rapid self-healing capacity at ambient temperature (healing efficiency > 85 % within 10 h), which stand out among the reported cases. Furthermore, thanks to the action of zinc ions, ZIWPU2 shows excellent antibacterial ability with an antibacterial rate of 97.2 % against E. coli and 98.5 % against S. aureus. Particularly, the CDs are incorporated into the WPU matrix to prepare photoluminescence materials, and their crack diagnosis and anti-counterfeiting functions are further demonstrated. Finally, we employ versatile emulsions to fabricate the leather coating. The dual dynamic bonds can significantly improve the adhesion and waterproofness of the coating and give it excellent room-temperature self-healing ability. Importantly, the introduction of CDs can effectively prevent crack propagation in leather coatings and ensure the authenticity of products.
